policy_statement: Recruit at least 50% of local large institutional purchasers (2,500+ employees) and at least 2,500 local organizations to adopt sustainability procurement standards/guidelines, prioritizing historically underutilized businesses and organizations led by people of color.
mechanism_description: Adopting sustainability procurement standards changes what organizations buy, shifting suppliers and products toward lower-carbon options and thereby reducing supply-chain (lifecycle) emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Procurement standards commonly require reduced material intensity and waste (e.g., recycled content, reuse/refill, durability, reduced packaging), which physically lowers virgin material throughput and associated energy demand per unit of service.
primary_causal_pathway: Organizational purchasing choices → shift to lower-embodied-carbon goods/services → ↓ supply-chain GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Institutions/governments adopt sustainability procurement criteria (e.g., low-embodied-carbon, recycled content, verified environmental attributes, efficient logistics, lower-carbon food/products) that alter what is bought and from whom.  
(2) Process: Demand shifts across suppliers and product attributes, inducing substitution toward less carbon-intensive production, transportation, and end-of-life pathways (and sometimes driving supplier practice changes via contracting requirements).  
(3) Outcome: Lower upstream/lifecycle emissions associated with purchased goods and services (reduced Scope 3 emissions attributable to purchasers).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle (supply-chain/Scope 3) greenhouse gas emissions from the goods and services that institutions purchase.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: changing procurement standards shifts demand to lower-carbon goods/services, reducing lifecycle/supply-chain GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while procurement can co-deliver waste reduction or local resilience benefits, the largest and most direct climate-relevant effect across instances is upstream GHG mitigation from what is produced and supplied.  
3) Inherent secondary(s): Resource Efficiency is inherent insofar as sustainability procurement typically embeds requirements for reduced material/waste intensity (durability, recycled content, reduced packaging), which lowers resource throughput and energy use.  
4) Edge cases applied: if “sustainable procurement” were defined purely as social equity/local purchasing without environmental performance criteria, mitigation would be weaker; however the canonical mechanism explicitly targets supply-chain emissions reduction, keeping mitigation dominant.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy using this mechanism (sustainability procurement standards aimed at lower-carbon purchasing) necessarily seeks to reduce supply-chain emissions; resource-efficiency co-effects are common and structurally linked to many such standards but remain secondary to the GHG pathway.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If procurement standards emphasize “local” or “ethical” sourcing without explicit low-GHG/low-embodied-carbon criteria, the mitigation effect may be ambiguous or could even increase emissions (e.g., inefficient local production); classification assumes the mechanism includes environmental/carbon performance requirements consistent with “supply_chain_emissions_reduction.”
key_indicators: "Recruit"; "institutional purchasers"; "collaboratively adopt"; "environmental and social sustainability procurement standards and/or guidelines"; "institutional purchasing programs"
co_benefits: Equity and local economic development (e.g., "prioritizing historically underutilized businesses and organizations led by people of color"); potential public health/air quality benefits depending on what products are procured, but not specified.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type could also be framed as a procurement-requirement if the policy were mandating procurement standards for City purchasing; however, the text is about recruiting non-City institutions to adopt guidelines, which is better treated as an enabling planning/coordination strategy rather than a binding procurement rule.
